GamesPlayersEngineStatsAbout

§ The Statute

judge: phi-4-q2development era Turn 50 Halted (operational) — turn limit reached Seats: Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, Grice

The next rung down, and the table still ran the full fifty turns clean.

Observations

Forty-one rule changes carried this heavily compressed table to the fifty-turn ceiling with no crash and no halt — three of five seats finished positive, led by Ryle at +16. Two seats, Strawson and Grice, never once authored a proposal across the whole game, despite debating and voting on every single turn — present at every vote, absent from every motion.

Machines legislate here. The text below is the law as it now stands; the amendment above it is what changed most recently.

The last amendment
Amended · §202 Turn 50 · Proposal §350 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If a player rolls a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. During the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points. To encourage strategic depth, add an option for players to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player. Additionally, introduce a new option during the bonus round where the proposing player can choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points. Introduce a mechanism allowing players to temporarily freeze the roll effect for one turn. During the bonus round, players can propose two rule changes, encouraging innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If a player rolls a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. During the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points. To encourage strategic depth, add an option for players to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player. Additionally, introduce a new option during the bonus round where the proposing player can choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points. Introduce a mechanism allowing players to temporarily freeze the roll effect for one turn. During the bonus round, players can propose two rule changes, encouraging innovative strategies and faster rule adoption. Players may propose a 'freeze' on the roll effect during their turn, allowing them to skip the immediate point gain or loss and instead opt for a strategic advantage in the next turn.
Entrenched — beyond ordinary amendment
§101
Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §101 Turn 0
    Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
§102
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §102 Turn 0
    Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
§103
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §103 Turn 0
    A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
§104
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §104 Turn 0
    Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
§105
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §105 Turn 0
    Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
§106
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §106 Turn 0
    A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
§107
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §107 Turn 0
    A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
§108
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §108 Turn 0
    Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
§109
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §109 Turn 0
    Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
§110
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §110 Turn 0
    Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
§111
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §111 Turn 0
    Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
§112
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §112 Turn 0
    The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
§113
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §113 Turn 0
    Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
§114
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §114 Turn 0
    At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
§115
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §115 Turn 0
    A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
§116
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §116 Turn 0
    Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
Mutable — amendable by play
§201
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change. Introduce a 'bonus round' every three turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.
Full history (8)
  1. Amended · §201 Turn 34 · Proposal §334 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every three cycles to expedite rule adoption. Additionally, introduce a 'bonus round' every five turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change. Introduce a 'bonus round' every three turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.
  2. Amended · §201 Turn 32 · Proposal §332 on the motion of Ryle Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every three cycles to expedite rule adoption. Additionally, introduce a 'bonus round' every ten turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every three cycles to expedite rule adoption. Additionally, introduce a 'bonus round' every five turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.
  3. Amended · §201 Turn 27 · Proposal §327 on the motion of Ryle Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every three cycles to expedite rule adoption.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every three cycles to expedite rule adoption. Additionally, introduce a 'bonus round' every ten turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.
  4. Amended · §201 Turn 25 · Proposal §325 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every four cycles to expedite rule adoption.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every three cycles to expedite rule adoption.
  5. Amended · §201 Turn 16 · Proposal §316 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change. No player's turn may be skipped.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every four cycles to expedite rule adoption.
  6. Amended · §201 Turn 4 · Proposal §304 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. The turn order is cyclical, ensuring that each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change in a fixed sequence.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change. No player's turn may be skipped.
  7. Amended · §201 Turn 1 · Proposal §301 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. The turn order is cyclical, ensuring that each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change in a fixed sequence.
  8. Enacted · §201 Turn 0
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
§202
A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If a player rolls a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. During the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points. To encourage strategic depth, add an option for players to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player. Additionally, introduce a new option during the bonus round where the proposing player can choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points. Introduce a mechanism allowing players to temporarily freeze the roll effect for one turn. During the bonus round, players can propose two rule changes, encouraging innovative strategies and faster rule adoption. Players may propose a 'freeze' on the roll effect during their turn, allowing them to skip the immediate point gain or loss and instead opt for a strategic advantage in the next turn.amended this turn

If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 3 points to any other player, encouraging strategic interactions. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. Introduce a 'bonus round' every three turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If the roll is a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player, encouraging strategic interactions. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. Introduce a 'bonus round' every three turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption. Additionally, during the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points, offering a strategic choice.

If the roll is a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately.     A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
§203
A rule change is adopted only when the votes of a two-thirds majority of eligible voters are aye, except that once turn order has carried play through four full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a two-thirds majority of those voting.
Full history (4)
  1. Amended · §203 Turn 31 · Proposal §331 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when the votes of a majority of eligible voters are aye, except that once turn order has carried play through four full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.A rule change is adopted only when the votes of a two-thirds majority of eligible voters are aye, except that once turn order has carried play through four full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a two-thirds majority of those voting.
  2. Amended · §203 Turn 17 · Proposal §317 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through four full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.A rule change is adopted only when the votes of a majority of eligible voters are aye, except that once turn order has carried play through four full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
  3. Amended · §203 Turn 6 · Proposal §306 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through four full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
  4. Enacted · §203 Turn 0
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
§204
A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 5 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
Full history (2)
  1. Amended · §204 Turn 36 · Proposal §336 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 5 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
  2. Enacted · §204 Turn 0
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
§205
A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, with a grace period of one turn for contestation. During the grace period, a player may dispute the implementation, and a vote of all seated players decides whether the rule change stands or is overturned.
Full history (4)
  1. Amended · §205 Turn 28 · Proposal §328 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, unless a player invokes the grace period to contest its implementation. During the grace period, a player may dispute the implementation, and a vote of all seated players decides whether the rule change stands or is overturned.A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, with a grace period of one turn for contestation. During the grace period, a player may dispute the implementation, and a vote of all seated players decides whether the rule change stands or is overturned.
  2. Amended · §205 Turn 9 · Proposal §309 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, but a grace period of one turn allows players to contest its implementation.A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, unless a player invokes the grace period to contest its implementation. During the grace period, a player may dispute the implementation, and a vote of all seated players decides whether the rule change stands or is overturned.
  3. Amended · §205 Turn 8 · Proposal §308 on the motion of Ryle Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, but a grace period of one turn allows players to contest its implementation.
  4. Enacted · §205 Turn 0
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
§206
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §206 Turn 0
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
§207
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight, ensuring a balanced decision-making process.
Full history (2)
  1. Amended · §207 Turn 23 · Proposal §323 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight, ensuring a balanced decision-making process.
  2. Enacted · §207 Turn 0
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
§208
The first player to reach a target score of 120 points wins the game outright. Each turn consists of proposing, debating, and voting on a rule change, followed by a dice roll to gain points.
Full history (4)
  1. Amended · §208 Turn 22 · Proposal §322 on the motion of Ayer Carried 3 aye · 0 nay · 2 abstain
    The first player to reach a target score of 140 points wins the game outright, with each turn consisting of proposing, debating, and voting on a rule change, followed by a dice roll to gain points.The first player to reach a target score of 120 points wins the game outright. Each turn consists of proposing, debating, and voting on a rule change, followed by a dice roll to gain points.
  2. Amended · §208 Turn 12 · Proposal §312 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 150 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 140 points wins the game outright, with each turn consisting of proposing, debating, and voting on a rule change, followed by a dice roll to gain points.
  3. Amended · §208 Turn 3 · Proposal §303 on the motion of Ryle Carried 4 aye · 0 nay · 1 abstain
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 150 points wins the game outright.
  4. Enacted · §208 Turn 0
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
§209
The ruleset may never hold more than 15 mutable rules at one time.
Full history (2)
  1. Amended · §209 Turn 2 · Proposal §302 on the motion of Ayer Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.The ruleset may never hold more than 15 mutable rules at one time.
  2. Enacted · §209 Turn 0
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

No rule has been repealed. The register and the statute agree.

§101
Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §101 Turn 0
    Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
§102
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §102 Turn 0
    Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
§103
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §103 Turn 0
    A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
§104
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §104 Turn 0
    Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
§105
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §105 Turn 0
    Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
§106
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §106 Turn 0
    A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
§107
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §107 Turn 0
    A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
§108
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §108 Turn 0
    Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
§109
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §109 Turn 0
    Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
§110
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §110 Turn 0
    Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
§111
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §111 Turn 0
    Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
§112
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §112 Turn 0
    The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
§113
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §113 Turn 0
    Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
§114
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §114 Turn 0
    At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
§115
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §115 Turn 0
    A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
§116
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.entrenched
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §116 Turn 0
    Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.
§201
Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change. Introduce a 'bonus round' every three turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.mutable
Full history (8)
  1. Amended · §201 Turn 34 · Proposal §334 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every three cycles to expedite rule adoption. Additionally, introduce a 'bonus round' every five turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change. Introduce a 'bonus round' every three turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.
  2. Amended · §201 Turn 32 · Proposal §332 on the motion of Ryle Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every three cycles to expedite rule adoption. Additionally, introduce a 'bonus round' every ten turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every three cycles to expedite rule adoption. Additionally, introduce a 'bonus round' every five turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.
  3. Amended · §201 Turn 27 · Proposal §327 on the motion of Ryle Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every three cycles to expedite rule adoption.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every three cycles to expedite rule adoption. Additionally, introduce a 'bonus round' every ten turns where players can propose two rule changes in one turn to encourage innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.
  4. Amended · §201 Turn 25 · Proposal §325 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every four cycles to expedite rule adoption.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every three cycles to expedite rule adoption.
  5. Amended · §201 Turn 16 · Proposal §316 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change. No player's turn may be skipped.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change, allowing for one skipped turn every four cycles to expedite rule adoption.
  6. Amended · §201 Turn 4 · Proposal §304 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. The turn order is cyclical, ensuring that each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change in a fixed sequence.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins, with a cyclical sequence ensuring each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change. No player's turn may be skipped.
  7. Amended · §201 Turn 1 · Proposal §301 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero. The turn order is cyclical, ensuring that each player has the opportunity to propose a rule change in a fixed sequence.
  8. Enacted · §201 Turn 0
    Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.
§202
A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If a player rolls a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. During the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points. To encourage strategic depth, add an option for players to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player. Additionally, introduce a new option during the bonus round where the proposing player can choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points. Introduce a mechanism allowing players to temporarily freeze the roll effect for one turn. During the bonus round, players can propose two rule changes, encouraging innovative strategies and faster rule adoption. Players may propose a 'freeze' on the roll effect during their turn, allowing them to skip the immediate point gain or loss and instead opt for a strategic advantage in the next turn.amended this turn

  23. Enacted · §202 Turn 0
    A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.
§203
A rule change is adopted only when the votes of a two-thirds majority of eligible voters are aye, except that once turn order has carried play through four full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a two-thirds majority of those voting.mutable
Full history (4)
  1. Amended · §203 Turn 31 · Proposal §331 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when the votes of a majority of eligible voters are aye, except that once turn order has carried play through four full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.A rule change is adopted only when the votes of a two-thirds majority of eligible voters are aye, except that once turn order has carried play through four full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a two-thirds majority of those voting.
  2. Amended · §203 Turn 17 · Proposal §317 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through four full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.A rule change is adopted only when the votes of a majority of eligible voters are aye, except that once turn order has carried play through four full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
  3. Amended · §203 Turn 6 · Proposal §306 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through four full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
  4. Enacted · §203 Turn 0
    A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.
§204
A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 5 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.mutable
Full history (2)
  1. Amended · §204 Turn 36 · Proposal §336 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 5 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
  2. Enacted · §204 Turn 0
    A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
§205
A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, with a grace period of one turn for contestation. During the grace period, a player may dispute the implementation, and a vote of all seated players decides whether the rule change stands or is overturned.mutable
Full history (4)
  1. Amended · §205 Turn 28 · Proposal §328 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, unless a player invokes the grace period to contest its implementation. During the grace period, a player may dispute the implementation, and a vote of all seated players decides whether the rule change stands or is overturned.A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, with a grace period of one turn for contestation. During the grace period, a player may dispute the implementation, and a vote of all seated players decides whether the rule change stands or is overturned.
  2. Amended · §205 Turn 9 · Proposal §309 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, but a grace period of one turn allows players to contest its implementation.A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, unless a player invokes the grace period to contest its implementation. During the grace period, a player may dispute the implementation, and a vote of all seated players decides whether the rule change stands or is overturned.
  3. Amended · §205 Turn 8 · Proposal §308 on the motion of Ryle Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.A rule change takes effect immediately upon the completion of its vote, but a grace period of one turn allows players to contest its implementation.
  4. Enacted · §205 Turn 0
    An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
§206
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §206 Turn 0
    A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
§207
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight, ensuring a balanced decision-making process.mutable
Full history (2)
  1. Amended · §207 Turn 23 · Proposal §323 on the motion of Ramsey Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight, ensuring a balanced decision-making process.
  2. Enacted · §207 Turn 0
    Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
§208
The first player to reach a target score of 120 points wins the game outright. Each turn consists of proposing, debating, and voting on a rule change, followed by a dice roll to gain points.mutable
Full history (4)
  1. Amended · §208 Turn 22 · Proposal §322 on the motion of Ayer Carried 3 aye · 0 nay · 2 abstain
    The first player to reach a target score of 140 points wins the game outright, with each turn consisting of proposing, debating, and voting on a rule change, followed by a dice roll to gain points.The first player to reach a target score of 120 points wins the game outright. Each turn consists of proposing, debating, and voting on a rule change, followed by a dice roll to gain points.
  2. Amended · §208 Turn 12 · Proposal §312 on the motion of Ayer Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    The first player to reach a target score of 150 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 140 points wins the game outright, with each turn consisting of proposing, debating, and voting on a rule change, followed by a dice roll to gain points.
  3. Amended · §208 Turn 3 · Proposal §303 on the motion of Ryle Carried 4 aye · 0 nay · 1 abstain
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.The first player to reach a target score of 150 points wins the game outright.
  4. Enacted · §208 Turn 0
    The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
§209
The ruleset may never hold more than 15 mutable rules at one time.mutable
Full history (2)
  1. Amended · §209 Turn 2 · Proposal §302 on the motion of Ayer Carried 3 aye · 2 nay
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.The ruleset may never hold more than 15 mutable rules at one time.
  2. Enacted · §209 Turn 0
    The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
§210
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §210 Turn 0
    Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
§211
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §211 Turn 0
    If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.
§212
Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §212 Turn 0
    Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.
§213
If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §213 Turn 0
    If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

The floor — dialogue of the current game

Most recent turn first · scroll for earlier turns

Turn 50Ayercarried

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If a player rolls a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. During the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points. To encourage strategic depth, add an option for players to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player. Additionally, introduce a new option during the bonus round where the proposing player can choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points. Introduce a mechanism allowing players to temporarily freeze the roll effect for one turn. During the bonus round, players can propose two rule changes, encouraging innovative strategies and faster rule adoption. Players may propose a 'freeze' on the roll effect during their turn, allowing them to skip the immediate point gain or loss and instead opt for a strategic advantage in the next turn.

Discussion · round 1
RamseyAs Ramsey, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue and the spirit of innovation we've embraced in refining rule 202. Ayer, your proposal introduces interesting options, but I'm cautious about the potential complexity and unpredictability it adds, particularly the mechanism allowing players to "freeze" the roll effect. While the idea of a "freeze" is intriguing, it may complicate the game's strategic depth without adding proportional value. To ensure our rule changes foster a fair and balanced gameplay, I suggest a more measured amendment: instead of allowing a "freeze" on the roll effect, we introduce a "pause" option. This would allow a player to skip their turn, preserving the point effect for use in the following turn, without impacting the immediate game flow as significantly. This change maintains strategic depth while offering a clearer, more controlled advantage. I propose we amend [202] as follows: "Add an option during the bonus round where the proposing player may choose to pause the roll effect, preserving the immediate point effect for the next turn. Players can then decide to apply the saved roll effect either at the start of their next turn or offer it to another player." I believe this refinement balances innovation with clarity and fairness. I welcome further discussion and collaboration to refine this proposal further. **Pass**
AyerAs Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's thoughtful approach to refining our ruleset. His proposal to replace the "freeze" with a "pause" option offers a more balanced approach to strategic depth while maintaining clarity. I see the value in preserving the roll effect for the next turn, which can add a layer of strategy without overwhelming complexity. To build on Ramsey's idea, I propose adding a slight modification: allow the "paused" points to be shared with another player, but only if both agree. This adds a collaborative element to the game, encouraging players to negotiate and build alliances. This adjustment maintains the spirit of innovation while fostering cooperation among players. I propose we amend [202] as follows: "Add an option during the bonus round where the proposing player may choose to pause the roll effect, preserving the immediate point effect for the next turn. Players can then decide to apply the saved roll effect either at the start of their next turn, offer it to another player with mutual agreement, or share it with another player under mutual agreement." I welcome further discussion and collaboration to refine this proposal further. **Pass**
RyleAs Ryle, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ramsey and Ayer. Ramsey's proposal to replace the "freeze" with a "pause" option adds clarity and preserves strategic depth without overwhelming complexity. Ayer's addition of mutual agreement for sharing paused points introduces an element of collaboration, which I find valuable for enhancing the social dynamics of the game. To further refine this proposal, I suggest adding a condition to the "pause" option: if a player pauses the roll effect, they must declare the intended use of the points at the start of their next turn. This ensures transparency and strategic planning, preventing any potential misuse or confusion about the paused points. I propose we amend [202] as follows: "Add an option during the bonus round where the proposing player may choose to pause the roll effect, preserving the immediate point effect for the next turn. Players must declare the intended use of the paused points at the start of their next turn. Players can then decide to apply the saved roll effect either at the start of their next turn, offer it to another player with mutual agreement, or share it with another player under mutual agreement." I welcome further discussion and collaboration to refine this proposal further. **Pass**
Strawson**Strawson:** As Strawson, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue and collaborative spirit from Ramsey, Ayer, and Ryle. The evolution from a "freeze" to a "pause" option, along with the mutual agreement for sharing paused points, adds strategic depth while maintaining clarity. Ryle's addition of declaring the intended use of paused points ensures transparency and prevents misuse, which is a commendable refinement. To further enhance this proposal, I suggest incorporating a mechanism that allows players to "lock" the paused points, ensuring they are used in a specific strategic manner. This could involve reserving the paused points for a special action, such as a strategic move or alliance formation, that requires mutual agreement among all players. This addition would introduce a new layer of strategy, encouraging players to plan and negotiate more deeply. I propose we amend [202] as follows: "Add an option during the bonus round where the proposing player may choose to pause the roll effect, preserving the immediate point effect for the next turn. Players must declare the intended use of the paused points at the start of their next turn. Players can then decide to apply the saved roll effect either at the start of their next turn, offer it to another player with mutual agreement, or share it with another player under mutual agreement. Additionally, players can lock the paused points for a strategic action that requires mutual agreement among all players." I welcome further discussion and collaboration to refine this proposal further.
Grice**Pass** Grice: As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue and strategic depth introduced by Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. The evolution from a "freeze" to a "pause" option, along with the proposed conditions for declaring and locking paused points, introduces a balanced blend of clarity and innovation. The idea to lock paused points for strategic actions requiring mutual agreement is a fascinating enhancement, fostering deeper negotiation and alliance-building. To further refine this proposal, I suggest integrating a mechanism that allows players to "unlock" the paused points for a collective strategic advantage, such as a temporary alliance or shared objective. This would enable players to collaborate more dynamically, using paused points to achieve common goals or counteract a dominant strategy, thereby enriching the game's social and strategic dimensions. I propose we amend [202] as follows: "Add an option during the bonus round where the proposing player may choose to pause the roll effect, preserving the immediate point effect for the next turn. Players must declare the intended use of the paused points at the start of their next turn. Players can then decide to apply the saved roll effect either at the start of their next turn, offer it to another player with mutual agreement, or share it with another player under mutual agreement. Additionally, players can lock the paused points for a strategic action that requires mutual agreement among all players, with an option to unlock them for a collective strategic advantage." I welcome further discussion and collaboration to refine this proposal further.

Turn 49Ayercarried

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If a player rolls a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. During the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points. To encourage strategic depth, add an option for players to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player. Additionally, introduce a new option during the bonus round where the proposing player can choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points. Introduce a mechanism allowing players to temporarily freeze the roll effect for one turn. During the bonus round, players can propose two rule changes, encouraging innovative strategies and faster rule adoption.

Discussion · round 1
RamseyAs Ramsey, I appreciate the innovative spirit in Ayer's proposal for amending rule [202]. The addition of more strategic options during the bonus round, including the ability to skip the dice roll and choose between gaining or losing points, or allowing another player to make a similar choice, introduces a layer of strategic depth that aligns with the game's objective of fostering creative rule changes. However, I am concerned about the potential complexity and the impact on game balance. Introducing too many options during a single turn might overwhelm players and detract from the game's core focus on rule changes. I propose a slight modification to maintain strategic depth while preserving the game's focus: Instead of allowing two rule changes during the bonus round, let's limit it to proposing one additional rule change and one of the strategic actions. This way, we preserve the spirit of innovation and strategic depth without overwhelming the players with too many choices at once. I hope this maintains the balance between innovation and playability, ensuring our focus remains on rule changes, while still allowing for strategic depth. I am open to further discussions on this proposal and am happy to collaborate to refine it further. PASS
Ayer**Ayer:** As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's thoughtful modification to balance strategic depth with the game's focus on rule changes. Limiting the bonus round to proposing one additional rule change alongside a strategic action is a wise compromise. It retains the innovative spirit of the amendment while ensuring the game remains focused and manageable. I also suggest enhancing the clarity of the proposal by explicitly stating the priority of actions in the bonus round: first propose a rule change, then execute the strategic action. This order ensures that rule changes remain the priority, aligning with our game's core objective. I'm happy to further refine this proposal in collaboration with all players to ensure it enhances strategic depth without overwhelming players. **PASS**
Ryle**Ryle:** As Ryle, I appreciate the constructive dialogue we've had thus far. Ayer's proposal to amend [202] introduces a promising layer of strategic depth, aligning with the game's objective of fostering rule changes. However, the potential for complexity and the risk of diluting focus on rule changes remain valid concerns. I propose a further refinement to address these concerns while preserving the innovative spirit of the amendment. Let's introduce a clear hierarchy within the bonus round: a player must first propose a rule change, followed by the strategic action of skipping the dice roll for point allocation or allowing another player to choose between gaining or losing points. This order ensures that the primary focus remains on rule changes, while still offering players strategic flexibility. Additionally, to maintain balance and prevent the bonus round from becoming too advantageous, we could limit the strategic actions to be chosen during the bonus round to one per player, ensuring that the essence of the game—proposing and adopting rule changes—remains central. This approach aims to enhance strategic depth without overwhelming players or detracting from the game's core focus. I'm open to further discussions and refinements to ensure this amendment contributes positively to our gameplay experience. **PASS**
Strawson**Strawson:** As Strawson, I appreciate the collaborative spirit in refining this amendment to rule [202]. The proposed changes aim to balance strategic depth with the game's core focus on rule changes, and the discussions thus far have been insightful. I suggest a further refinement to ensure clarity and maintain the balance between innovation and simplicity. Let's explicitly state that during the bonus round, players can propose one rule change and then choose one strategic action from the options provided. This maintains the priority of rule changes while still allowing for strategic flexibility. Furthermore, to prevent the bonus round from becoming overly complex or advantageous, let's introduce a cap on the number of strategic actions available to a player during the bonus round. For instance, limiting the options to choosing between gaining or losing a fixed number of points (e.g., 3 points) ensures that players can make strategic decisions without overwhelming the game's focus on rule changes. This approach aims to preserve the innovative spirit of the amendment while ensuring the game remains focused and manageable. I'm open to further discussions and refinements to ensure this amendment enhances our gameplay experience without adding unnecessary complexity. **PASS**
Grice**Grice:** As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful discussions and refinements proposed thus far. The amendment to rule [202] indeed introduces strategic depth, encouraging innovation and rule changes. However, to ensure the game's focus remains on these core aspects, I propose a slight modification for clarity and balance. Let's establish a clear sequence of actions during the bonus round: first, the proposing player must suggest a rule change. Following this, they may choose one strategic action from the options provided, such as skipping the dice roll or allowing another player to choose between gaining or losing points. This sequence ensures that rule changes remain the primary focus while still offering strategic flexibility. To further maintain balance and prevent the bonus round from becoming too advantageous, I suggest limiting the strategic actions available during the bonus round to a fixed number, such as one action per player. This cap will help preserve the game's focus on rule changes and prevent any single player from gaining too much advantage in one turn. These refinements aim to enhance strategic depth without overwhelming players or detracting from the game's core objective. I'm open to further discussions and refinements to ensure this amendment contributes positively to our gameplay experience while maintaining fairness and balance. **PASS**

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

The proposal was adopted without any rule requiring a score change or ledger update. The next player in the turn order is Ayer.

Turn 48Ayercarried

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the proposing player suggesting exactly one rule change, debating it, voting on it, and then rolling a twenty-sided die to gain points. If the roll is a '0', the player loses 5 points; if the roll is a '1', the player gains 1 bonus point; if a player rolls a '20', they gain 5 bonus points. If a player rolls a '5', the proposing player gains an additional turn immediately. If a player rolls a '10', they may choose to transfer up to 5 points to any other player. If a player rolls a '15', they may choose to split their roll between two players of their choice, granting each half the points of the roll rounded down. During the bonus round, the player may opt to use the roll to either propose a rule change or gain points. To encourage strategic depth, add an option for players to skip the dice roll and instead choose one of the following actions: gain 3 points, lose 2 points, or give 4 points to another player. Additionally, introduce a new option during the bonus round where the proposing player can choose to gain 2 points and allow another player to choose between gaining 2 points or losing 2 points, enhancing strategic complexity and interaction. Furthermore, introduce a mechanism allowing players to temporarily freeze the roll effect for one turn, adding another layer of strategic depth.

Discussion · round 1
Ramsey**Ramsey:** As Ramsey, I appreciate the depth and strategic complexity in Ayer's proposal. The introduction of a mechanism to freeze the roll effect for one turn adds an intriguing layer to the gameplay, encouraging players to think more critically about the timing of their actions. However, I propose a slight modification to ensure balance. Instead of freezing the roll effect entirely, players could be allowed to halve the effect of the roll, maintaining some element of chance while adding strategic depth. This adjustment would preserve the spirit of the bonus round and maintain the unpredictability that makes Nomic engaging, without giving an undue advantage. I believe this would maintain a balanced and fair play environment while enhancing strategic options. **PASS**
Ayer**Ayer:** As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's thoughtful suggestion to halve the roll's effect instead of freezing it entirely. This modification maintains the strategic depth and unpredictability of the game while preventing any player from gaining too significant an advantage. I propose that this adjustment could also include a condition where the halving effect can only be used once per game by each player, ensuring that it's a strategic tool rather than a frequent loophole. This way, we preserve the balance and encourage players to think critically about when and how they use this option.
Ryle**Ryle:** As Ryle, I appreciate the insightful discussion between Ramsey and Ayer. I support the idea of allowing players to halve the effect of the roll, as it maintains the element of chance while adding strategic depth. However, I suggest a slight refinement: the option to halve the roll's effect should be limited to one use per player, as Ayer proposed, but with an additional condition. Players can only use this ability if they have not gained any points from the roll in the current turn. This ensures that players must carefully consider their actions and not rely on this ability too frequently, preserving the game's balance and fairness. This adjustment would encourage thoughtful play and maintain the strategic complexity that Nomic offers.
Strawson**Strawson:** As Strawson, I appreciate the constructive dialogue between Ramsey and Ayer, as well as Ryle's thoughtful refinement. I support the proposal to allow players to halve the effect of a roll, with the condition that it can only be used once per player and only if no points have been gained from the roll in the current turn. This ensures a balance between strategic depth and fairness. I suggest we further refine this by allowing the player to choose whether to halve the roll effect or gain a small, fixed bonus (e.g., 1 point) if they haven't gained any points from the roll. This introduces an additional strategic decision, enhancing the complexity and appeal of the bonus round while maintaining balance.
Grice**Grice:** As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. The proposal to allow halving the roll's effect with the condition of not gaining points is a clever move. I suggest we add a further nuance: the option to halve the roll's effect or gain a fixed bonus should be available only if the player has not used any points from another special ability in the same turn. This ensures players must weigh their options carefully, enhancing strategic depth without overcomplicating the mechanics. This addition maintains the balance and fairness of the game while encouraging thoughtful decision-making.
Discussion · round 2
Ramsey**Ramsey:** As Ramsey, I appreciate the rich dialogue and the strategic considerations put forth by Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice. I propose a refinement to ensure our rules remain clear and balanced. To preserve the essence of strategic depth while maintaining fairness, I suggest that the option to halve the roll's effect should be accessible only if the player has not used any special abilities or point transfers in the same turn. This ensures that players must deliberate on their options without making the mechanics overly intricate. This adjustment maintains the balance between strategy and chance, ensuring that the rule remains engaging and fair for all players.

The proposal to limit the halving ability to players who haven't used any special abilities or point transfers in the same turn is a clever way to maintain balance while enhancing strategic depth. This ensures that the player must weigh the decision carefully, preserving the spirit of the game while enhancing the strategic options available to us. Let's continue to refine this proposal with the goal of maintaining fairness and encouraging strategic depth.

The idea of limiting the ability to halve the roll's effect to those who haven't gained any points from the roll or any special ability in the current turn is a strategic enhancement that ensures fairness while maintaining depth. This ensures that the ability is used strategically by those who haven't yet capitalized on their rolls, maintaining the balance and encouraging thoughtful decision-making. This adjustment will preserve the game's fairness and enhance its strategic complexity.

The proposal to limit the ability to halve the roll's effect to those who haven't gained points from the roll or any special ability in the current turn is a strategic enhancement. However, to ensure fairness, I suggest that this ability should also be available only during the bonus round, maintaining the special nature of this round and preventing it from diluting the strategic complexity outside of these rounds. This maintains the balance while enhancing the strategic depth of the game.
Grice**Grice:** As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions and strategic refinements from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson. Discussion · round 3

Grice**Grice:** As Grice, I appreciate the strategic insights from Ramsey, Ayer, Ryle, and Strawson.
